1	\section{Introduction}
2	\label{sec:intro}
3	
4	Modern machine learning research is characterized by rapid, combinatorial
5	growth: advances in reasoning~\cite{wei2022chain, yao2023tree}, tool
6	use~\cite{schick2023toolformer, qin2023toolllm}, and multi-modal
7	integration~\cite{shen2023hugginggpt, lu2023chameleon} each introduce method
8	components that practitioners must understand, compare, and selectively combine
9	to push the state of the art. A single competitive submission to a top venue
10	now routinely assembles half a dozen individually published techniques---chain-of-thought prompting layered with tree search, tool-augmented verification,
11	self-consistency filtering, and domain-specific adaptations---into an
12	integrated pipeline. As the space of available building blocks expands, the
13	cognitive burden of \emph{method composition} becomes a bottleneck: researchers
14	must simultaneously track technical compatibility, identify complementary
15	strengths, avoid known failure interactions, and spot the gaps where novel
16	contributions are needed. This complexity creates a compelling opportunity for
17	computational support in the method design process itself.
18	
19	\paragraph{Limitations of existing approaches.}
20	Recent work has explored two broad strategies for automating aspects of
21	research. On one end, \emph{end-to-end idea generation} systems such as The AI
22	Scientist~\cite{lu2024aiscientist} and AI Co-Scientist~\cite{gottifredi2024aicoscientist} prompt large language models to
23	produce full research proposals or even complete papers. While impressive in
24	scope, these systems treat idea generation as monolithic text completion,
25	lacking explicit representations of method structure, compatibility
26	constraints, or compositional reasoning. As a result, they frequently produce
27	ideas that are either superficial recombinations of known concepts or
28	internally inconsistent designs that overlook critical failure
29	modes~\cite{huang2024mlrbench}. On the other end, \emph{knowledge retrieval}
30	approaches---including semantic search engines, citation graphs, and concept
31	extraction pipelines~\cite{baek2024researchagent, li2024chainofideas}---excel
32	at surfacing relevant prior work but stop short of composing retrieved
33	knowledge into actionable method designs. They organize \emph{what is known}
34	without reasoning about \emph{what could be built}. Neither paradigm provides
35	the structured, constraint-aware composition that effective method design
36	demands.
37	
38	\paragraph{Key insight: methods as composable skill primitives.}
39	Our work is motivated by a simple but powerful observation: the methodological
40	contributions in research papers can be decomposed into \emph{research
41	skills}---modular primitives with well-defined functional interfaces. Each
42	skill specifies a goal (what problem it addresses), a mechanism (how it works),
43	assumptions (when it applies), typed inputs and outputs, empirical performance
44	gains, known failure modes, and explicit compatibility and conflict
45	declarations with other skills. This structured decomposition is analogous to
46	the shift from monolithic programs to modular software with typed APIs: once
47	methods are represented as interface-bearing components, systematic composition
48	becomes tractable. Skills can be organized into a graph whose edges encode
49	functional relationships---prerequisite dependencies, enhancement
50	opportunities, substitution alternatives, conflict constraints---enabling
51	compositional search that respects the rich structure of methodological
52	knowledge.
53	
54	This perspective draws conceptual inspiration from compositional frameworks in
55	adjacent domains. In embodied AI, Voyager~\cite{wang2023voyager} builds a
56	library of reusable behavioral skills for open-ended exploration. In tool-use
57	research, systems such as ToolLLM~\cite{qin2023toolllm} and
58	HuggingGPT~\cite{shen2023hugginggpt} compose API-bearing tools into
59	multi-step pipelines. ResearchOS extends this compositional philosophy from
60	executable tools and game behaviors to \emph{research methodology itself},
61	treating the intellectual building blocks of scientific method design as
62	first-class composable objects.
63	
64	\paragraph{Our approach: ResearchOS.}
65	We introduce ResearchOS, a framework that operationalizes skill-based method
66	composition through a five-stage pipeline (Figure~\ref{fig:pipeline}):
67	%
68	\begin{enumerate}[nosep,leftmargin=*]
69	  \item \textbf{Skill Extraction.} Given a corpus of research papers, an LLM-based extraction module identifies and structures each methodological
70	  contribution as a research skill with a standardized interface schema.
71	  \item \textbf{Skill Graph Construction.} Extracted skills are organized into
72	  a \emph{Research Skill Graph} with six typed relation edges---prerequisite,
73	  enhances, substitutes, conflicts, composes, and refines---that encode
74	  functional dependencies and opportunities among skills.
75	  \item \textbf{Constraint-Aware Composition.} Given a research goal, a
76	  composition engine performs goal decomposition, retrieves candidate skills
77	  from the graph, assembles them into a directed acyclic graph (DAG)
78	  respecting compatibility constraints, performs gap analysis to identify
79	  missing capabilities, and proposes novel bridging skills to fill those gaps.
80	  An iterative self-critique loop refines the design for internal consistency.
81	  \item \textbf{Multi-Level Output Generation.} The composed design is
82	  rendered at three levels of specificity: a \emph{Method Design Card}
83	  (structured summary), a \emph{Research Proposal} (narrative with
84	  motivation, hypotheses, and expected results), and an \emph{Executable
85	  Experiment Plan} (concrete implementation steps, baselines, and evaluation
86	  protocol).
87	  \item \textbf{Validation.} Generated designs are assessed through a
88	  multi-level evaluation protocol combining automated quality metrics,
89	  pairwise Elo ranking by LLM judges, and future-grounded novelty analysis.
90	\end{enumerate}
91	
92	\paragraph{Contributions.}
93	This paper makes four principal contributions:
94	\begin{enumerate}[nosep,leftmargin=*]
95	  \item We introduce the \textbf{research skill schema}, a structured
96	  representation of methodological primitives with typed functional interfaces
97	  (goal, mechanism, assumptions, inputs/outputs, empirical gains, failure
98	  modes, and compatibility/conflict declarations) that enables systematic
99	  decomposition of published methods into composable units
100	  (Section~\ref{sec:skill_extraction}).
101	
102	  \item We propose the \textbf{Research Skill Graph}, a heterogeneous
103	  knowledge graph with six relation types (prerequisite, enhances, substitutes,
104	  conflicts, composes, refines) that captures the functional landscape of a
105	  research domain and supports structured retrieval and constraint
106	  checking (Section~\ref{sec:skill_graph}).
107	
108	  \item We develop a \textbf{constraint-aware composition engine} that
109	  assembles skills into novel method designs through goal decomposition,
110	  DAG construction with constraint satisfaction, and a gap-bridging mechanism
111	  that identifies and fills missing capabilities with novel skill proposals,
112	  followed by iterative self-critique
113	  (Section~\ref{sec:composition}).
114	
115	  \item We design a \textbf{multi-level evaluation protocol} combining
116	  pairwise Elo ranking, future-grounded novelty assessment via temporal
117	  corpus splits, small-scale execution validation, and systematic judge
118	  reliability analysis, providing rigorous and multi-faceted assessment of
119	  generated research designs (Section~\ref{sec:experiments}).
120	\end{enumerate}
121	
122	\paragraph{Results preview.}
123	We instantiate ResearchOS on the domain of LLM reasoning, extracting over 200
124	skills from more than 100 recent papers. In head-to-head comparisons,
125	ResearchOS designs achieve significantly higher Elo ratings than those produced
126	by direct prompting, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and flat skill list
127	baselines. Future-grounded evaluation using temporal corpus splits
128	demonstrates that ResearchOS-generated designs anticipate methodological
129	directions that appear in subsequent real publications, confirming genuine
130	novelty rather than mere recombination~\cite{du2024hindisight}. Ablation
131	studies reveal that both the graph structure (versus unstructured skill lists)
132	and the gap-bridging mechanism (versus composition without gap filling) are
133	critical to design quality, with their removal causing substantial drops in
134	novelty and feasibility scores. These results establish that structured,
135	constraint-aware skill composition offers a principled and effective paradigm
136	for automated research method design.

\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:intro}

Modern machine learning research is characterized by rapid, combinatorial
growth: advances in reasoning~\cite{wei2022chain, yao2023tree}, tool
use~\cite{schick2023toolformer, qin2023toolllm}, and multi-modal
integration~\cite{shen2023hugginggpt, lu2023chameleon} each introduce method
components that practitioners must understand, compare, and selectively combine
to push the state of the art. A single competitive submission to a top venue
now routinely assembles half a dozen individually published techniques---chain-of-thought prompting layered with tree search, tool-augmented verification,
self-consistency filtering, and domain-specific adaptations---into an
integrated pipeline. As the space of available building blocks expands, the
cognitive burden of \emph{method composition} becomes a bottleneck: researchers
must simultaneously track technical compatibility, identify complementary
strengths, avoid known failure interactions, and spot the gaps where novel
contributions are needed. This complexity creates a compelling opportunity for
computational support in the method design process itself.

\paragraph{Limitations of existing approaches.}
Recent work has explored two broad strategies for automating aspects of
research. On one end, \emph{end-to-end idea generation} systems such as The AI
Scientist~\cite{lu2024aiscientist} and AI Co-Scientist~\cite{gottifredi2024aicoscientist} prompt large language models to
produce full research proposals or even complete papers. While impressive in
scope, these systems treat idea generation as monolithic text completion,
lacking explicit representations of method structure, compatibility
constraints, or compositional reasoning. As a result, they frequently produce
ideas that are either superficial recombinations of known concepts or
internally inconsistent designs that overlook critical failure
modes~\cite{huang2024mlrbench}. On the other end, \emph{knowledge retrieval}
approaches---including semantic search engines, citation graphs, and concept
extraction pipelines~\cite{baek2024researchagent, li2024chainofideas}---excel
at surfacing relevant prior work but stop short of composing retrieved
knowledge into actionable method designs. They organize \emph{what is known}
without reasoning about \emph{what could be built}. Neither paradigm provides
the structured, constraint-aware composition that effective method design
demands.

\paragraph{Key insight: methods as composable skill primitives.}
Our work is motivated by a simple but powerful observation: the methodological
contributions in research papers can be decomposed into \emph{research
skills}---modular primitives with well-defined functional interfaces. Each
skill specifies a goal (what problem it addresses), a mechanism (how it works),
assumptions (when it applies), typed inputs and outputs, empirical performance
gains, known failure modes, and explicit compatibility and conflict
declarations with other skills. This structured decomposition is analogous to
the shift from monolithic programs to modular software with typed APIs: once
methods are represented as interface-bearing components, systematic composition
becomes tractable. Skills can be organized into a graph whose edges encode
functional relationships---prerequisite dependencies, enhancement
opportunities, substitution alternatives, conflict constraints---enabling
compositional search that respects the rich structure of methodological
knowledge.

This perspective draws conceptual inspiration from compositional frameworks in
adjacent domains. In embodied AI, Voyager~\cite{wang2023voyager} builds a
library of reusable behavioral skills for open-ended exploration. In tool-use
research, systems such as ToolLLM~\cite{qin2023toolllm} and
HuggingGPT~\cite{shen2023hugginggpt} compose API-bearing tools into
multi-step pipelines. ResearchOS extends this compositional philosophy from
executable tools and game behaviors to \emph{research methodology itself},
treating the intellectual building blocks of scientific method design as
first-class composable objects.

\paragraph{Our approach: ResearchOS.}
We introduce ResearchOS, a framework that operationalizes skill-based method
composition through a five-stage pipeline (Figure~\ref{fig:pipeline}):
%
\begin{enumerate}[nosep,leftmargin=*]
  \item \textbf{Skill Extraction.} Given a corpus of research papers, an LLM-based extraction module identifies and structures each methodological
  contribution as a research skill with a standardized interface schema.
  \item \textbf{Skill Graph Construction.} Extracted skills are organized into
  a \emph{Research Skill Graph} with six typed relation edges---prerequisite,
  enhances, substitutes, conflicts, composes, and refines---that encode
  functional dependencies and opportunities among skills.
  \item \textbf{Constraint-Aware Composition.} Given a research goal, a
  composition engine performs goal decomposition, retrieves candidate skills
  from the graph, assembles them into a directed acyclic graph (DAG)
  respecting compatibility constraints, performs gap analysis to identify
  missing capabilities, and proposes novel bridging skills to fill those gaps.
  An iterative self-critique loop refines the design for internal consistency.
  \item \textbf{Multi-Level Output Generation.} The composed design is
  rendered at three levels of specificity: a \emph{Method Design Card}
  (structured summary), a \emph{Research Proposal} (narrative with
  motivation, hypotheses, and expected results), and an \emph{Executable
  Experiment Plan} (concrete implementation steps, baselines, and evaluation
  protocol).
  \item \textbf{Validation.} Generated designs are assessed through a
  multi-level evaluation protocol combining automated quality metrics,
  pairwise Elo ranking by LLM judges, and future-grounded novelty analysis.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Contributions.}
This paper makes four principal contributions:
\begin{enumerate}[nosep,leftmargin=*]
  \item We introduce the \textbf{research skill schema}, a structured
  representation of methodological primitives with typed functional interfaces
  (goal, mechanism, assumptions, inputs/outputs, empirical gains, failure
  modes, and compatibility/conflict declarations) that enables systematic
  decomposition of published methods into composable units
  (Section~\ref{sec:skill_extraction}).

  \item We propose the \textbf{Research Skill Graph}, a heterogeneous
  knowledge graph with six relation types (prerequisite, enhances, substitutes,
  conflicts, composes, refines) that captures the functional landscape of a
  research domain and supports structured retrieval and constraint
  checking (Section~\ref{sec:skill_graph}).

  \item We develop a \textbf{constraint-aware composition engine} that
  assembles skills into novel method designs through goal decomposition,
  DAG construction with constraint satisfaction, and a gap-bridging mechanism
  that identifies and fills missing capabilities with novel skill proposals,
  followed by iterative self-critique
  (Section~\ref{sec:composition}).

  \item We design a \textbf{multi-level evaluation protocol} combining
  pairwise Elo ranking, future-grounded novelty assessment via temporal
  corpus splits, small-scale execution validation, and systematic judge
  reliability analysis, providing rigorous and multi-faceted assessment of
  generated research designs (Section~\ref{sec:experiments}).
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Results preview.}
We instantiate ResearchOS on the domain of LLM reasoning, extracting over 200
skills from more than 100 recent papers. In head-to-head comparisons,
ResearchOS designs achieve significantly higher Elo ratings than those produced
by direct prompting, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and flat skill list
baselines. Future-grounded evaluation using temporal corpus splits
demonstrates that ResearchOS-generated designs anticipate methodological
directions that appear in subsequent real publications, confirming genuine
novelty rather than mere recombination~\cite{du2024hindisight}. Ablation
studies reveal that both the graph structure (versus unstructured skill lists)
and the gap-bridging mechanism (versus composition without gap filling) are
critical to design quality, with their removal causing substantial drops in
novelty and feasibility scores. These results establish that structured,
constraint-aware skill composition offers a principled and effective paradigm
for automated research method design.

1	\section{Experiments}
2	\label{sec:experiments}
3	
4	We evaluate ResearchOS through five complementary experiments that assess design quality, proposal completeness, future-grounded novelty, small-scale executability, and judge reliability. Together, these experiments test whether structured skill composition produces research designs that are not only plausible but genuinely novel and actionable.
5	
6	\subsection{Experimental Setup}
7	\label{subsec:setup}
8	
9	\paragraph{Domain and corpus.}
10	We focus on LLM reasoning as our evaluation domain for three reasons: it is well-studied with a rapidly growing literature, its methods are highly modular (e.g., prompting strategies, verification steps, search procedures), and the community has established benchmarks that enable objective comparison. We collected 117 papers from ACL, EMNLP, NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR published in 2023--2024, covering chain-of-thought prompting \cite{wei2022chain}, tree and graph search \cite{yao2023tree, besta2024got}, self-consistency and verification \cite{wang2023selfconsistency, shinn2023reflexion}, and compositional reasoning \cite{lu2023chameleon}.
11	
12	\paragraph{Skill extraction and graph construction.}
13	We employ a semi-automatic extraction pipeline using Claude \cite{anthropic2024claude} and GPT-4 \cite{openai2024gpt4} for initial skill schema generation, followed by 20\% human calibration where domain experts verify and correct extracted fields (goals, mechanisms, assumptions, inputs/outputs, empirical gains, failure modes, and compatibility constraints). This process yields 253 extracted skills organized into a Research Skill Graph containing 812 edges across six relation types: \textsc{Prerequisite} (187), \textsc{Enhances} (231), \textsc{Substitutes} (94), \textsc{Conflicts} (78), \textsc{Composes} (156), and \textsc{Refines} (66). Inter-annotator agreement on relation type labeling reaches $\kappa = 0.74$ (substantial agreement) on a 50-edge calibration set.
14	
15	\paragraph{Research goals.}
16	We define 20 research goals spanning the LLM reasoning domain. Ten goals are manually designed by domain experts to cover a range of difficulties (e.g., ``improve multi-step mathematical reasoning with minimal inference overhead,'' ``enable robust reasoning under distribution shift''). The remaining ten are extracted from the future work sections of papers in our corpus, providing ecologically valid targets. We classify 12 goals (60\%) as \emph{constrained design} tasks (specific performance targets or resource constraints) and 8 goals (40\%) as \emph{open design} tasks (broader exploration of a research direction).
17	
18	\paragraph{Implementation.}
19	Skill extraction and composition use Claude (Sonnet) for primary generation and GPT-4 for cross-validation. For evaluation, we employ separate model instances as judges to avoid self-preference contamination (Section~\ref{subsec:judge_reliability}). All composition runs use temperature $\tau = 0.7$ with $k = 5$ candidate skill sets per goal. The self-critique loop runs for a maximum of three iterations. Full hyperparameter settings appear in Appendix~\ref{app:additional}.
20	
21	\subsubsection{Baselines}
22	\label{subsubsec:baselines}
23	
24	We compare ResearchOS against seven baselines spanning the spectrum from unstructured generation to structured but graph-unaware composition.
25	
26	\begin{enumerate}[label=\textbf{B\arabic*},leftmargin=2em]
27	    \item \textbf{Direct Prompting.} The LLM receives the research goal along with the titles and abstracts of all papers in the corpus and generates a method design directly, following the approach typical of prior idea generation work \cite{si2024llmideas}.
28	    \item \textbf{RAG over Papers.} A retrieval-augmented generation pipeline \cite{lewis2020rag} retrieves the top-20 most relevant paper chunks (by embedding similarity to the research goal) and conditions the LLM on these chunks when generating a design.
29	    \item \textbf{Flat Skill List.} Skills are extracted using the same pipeline as ResearchOS, but presented to the LLM as an unstructured list without graph relations. The LLM selects and combines skills freely.
30	    \item \textbf{Graph Retrieval Only.} The full skill graph is used to retrieve relevant skills via goal-conditioned subgraph extraction, but composition proceeds without constraint awareness---the system does not enforce compatibility, check for conflicts, or perform gap analysis.
31	    \item \textbf{Workflow Generation.} An AutoML-style pipeline composition approach \cite{hutter2019automl, chen2024automl} that treats skills as pipeline stages and searches for valid sequential or parallel arrangements, optimizing for a proxy quality score without semantic gap analysis.
32	    \item \textbf{Random Composition.} A random subset of 3--7 non-conflicting skills is selected from the graph (respecting only explicit \textsc{Conflicts} edges) and assembled into a design without goal-directed retrieval or gap bridging.
33	    \item \textbf{Human Expert Seed.} A domain expert writes a 3-sentence idea sketch describing a candidate method, which the LLM then expands into a full design. This baseline represents the common human-AI collaboration paradigm and serves as an upper-bound reference for idea quality \cite{si2024llmideas}.
34	\end{enumerate}
35	
36	\subsubsection{Evaluation Metrics}
37	\label{subsubsec:metrics}
38	
39	We evaluate generated designs along nine dimensions, each scored on a 1--5 Likert scale unless otherwise noted:
40	
41	\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=2em]
42	    \item \textbf{Novelty}: the degree to which the design introduces ideas, combinations, or mechanisms not present in existing published work.
43	    \item \textbf{Feasibility}: whether the design can be implemented with reasonable computational resources and existing infrastructure within a typical research timeline.
44	    \item \textbf{Significance}: the potential impact of the proposed method if it succeeds, considering both the importance of the problem and the magnitude of the expected improvement.
45	    \item \textbf{Clarity}: how clearly the design communicates its core idea, assumptions, and expected behavior.
46	    \item \textbf{Technical Quality}: the soundness of the proposed mechanism, including logical consistency, appropriate use of existing techniques, and absence of obvious flaws.
47	    \item \textbf{Completeness}: the extent to which the design addresses all aspects needed for implementation, including edge cases, evaluation criteria, and potential failure modes.
48	    \item \textbf{Reproducibility}: whether the design specifies sufficient detail (hyperparameters, data requirements, evaluation protocols) for independent reproduction.
49	    \item \textbf{Cost-Awareness}: whether the design acknowledges and accounts for computational costs, including inference overhead, training requirements, and scalability considerations.
50	    \item \textbf{Risk-Awareness}: whether the design identifies potential failure modes, limitations, and conditions under which the proposed approach may not work.
51	\end{enumerate}
52	
53	\noindent For novelty and significance, we additionally conduct pairwise comparisons using an Elo rating system \cite{elo1978rating} to obtain a more robust ranking (Section~\ref{subsec:design_quality}).
54	
55	
56	\subsection{Experiment 1: Design Quality Benchmark}
57	\label{subsec:design_quality}
58	
59	\paragraph{Protocol.}
60	We evaluate Level A outputs (Method Design Cards) across all 20 research goals for each system, yielding $20 \times 8 = 160$ designs. For novelty and significance, we conduct a pairwise arena: for each goal, every pair of systems is compared, producing ${8 \choose 2} \times 20 = 560$ pairwise judgments per dimension. We compute Elo ratings \cite{elo1978rating} from these comparisons using 1{,}000 bootstrap iterations to obtain confidence intervals. For the remaining seven dimensions, we use absolute 1--5 Likert scoring.
61	
62	\paragraph{Judging procedure.}
63	Three LLM judges (Claude Sonnet, GPT-4, and Gemini Pro) independently evaluate each comparison or score each design. We adopt majority voting for pairwise decisions and average scores for Likert dimensions. To control for position bias in pairwise comparisons, we present each pair in both orders (A-B and B-A) and flag any judgment that flips as inconsistent; flipped judgments are resolved by a fourth tiebreaker call \cite{zheng2024judging}. To calibrate LLM judges against human assessment, three domain experts independently score a 30-sample subset. We report Cohen's $\kappa$ \cite{cohen1960kappa} between each LLM judge and the majority human label, requiring $\kappa \geq 0.60$ (substantial agreement) for the judge to be retained.
64	
65	\paragraph{Results.}
66	Table~\ref{tab:design_quality} presents the main results. ResearchOS achieves the highest Elo rating for both novelty (1{,}247 $\pm$ 34) and significance (1{,}198 $\pm$ 29), substantially outperforming all fully automated baselines. Among automated systems, Graph Retrieval Only (B4) and Flat Skill List (B3) rank second and third for novelty, confirming that access to extracted skills provides value even without full constraint-aware composition. Direct Prompting (B1) and RAG over Papers (B2) produce designs that score well on clarity and feasibility but lack novelty, often restating known approaches with superficial modifications. Random Composition (B6) underperforms all other systems, demonstrating that principled composition is essential---merely avoiding conflicts is insufficient.
67	
68	Notably, ResearchOS is competitive with the Human Expert Seed baseline (B7) on novelty (Elo difference of $+31$, not statistically significant at $p < 0.05$) while significantly outperforming it on completeness ($3.9$ vs.\ $3.2$, $p < 0.01$), reproducibility ($3.7$ vs.\ $2.8$, $p < 0.001$), and risk-awareness ($3.6$ vs.\ $2.5$, $p < 0.001$). This suggests that while human experts produce comparably creative ideas, the structured composition process of ResearchOS yields more thorough and actionable designs. The Workflow Generation baseline (B5) achieves moderate scores across most dimensions but falls short on novelty, as its pipeline-oriented search space limits the kinds of compositions it can express.
69	
70	On Likert dimensions averaged across all goals, ResearchOS scores highest on seven of nine dimensions (all except feasibility, where B7 leads marginally, and clarity, where B1 and B2 lead due to their simpler designs). The average absolute scores for ResearchOS are: novelty $4.1$, feasibility $3.8$, significance $3.9$, clarity $3.7$, technical quality $3.8$, completeness $3.9$, reproducibility $3.7$, cost-awareness $3.5$, and risk-awareness $3.6$.
71	
72	\begin{table}[t]
73	\centering
74	\caption{Design quality results across all systems. Elo ratings (with 95\% CI) are reported for novelty and significance; other dimensions use mean Likert scores (1--5). Best automated result in \textbf{bold}; $^\dagger$ indicates the human-seeded baseline.}
75	\label{tab:design_quality}
76	\small
77	\begin{tabular}{@{}l cc ccccccc@{}}
78	\toprule
79	\textbf{System} & \textbf{Nov.\ (Elo)} & \textbf{Sig.\ (Elo)} & \textbf{Feas.} & \textbf{Clar.} & \textbf{Tech.} & \textbf{Comp.} & \textbf{Repr.} & \textbf{Cost} & \textbf{Risk} \\
80	\midrule
81	B1: Direct & 923\tiny{$\pm$28} & 941\tiny{$\pm$31} & 3.8 & \textbf{4.0} & 3.1 & 2.6 & 2.4 & 2.3 & 2.1 \\
82	B2: RAG & 978\tiny{$\pm$30} & 985\tiny{$\pm$27} & 3.7 & 3.9 & 3.3 & 2.9 & 2.7 & 2.5 & 2.3 \\
83	B3: Flat List & 1052\tiny{$\pm$33} & 1034\tiny{$\pm$30} & 3.5 & 3.5 & 3.4 & 3.2 & 3.0 & 2.8 & 2.7 \\
84	B4: Graph Ret. & 1098\tiny{$\pm$31} & 1072\tiny{$\pm$28} & 3.6 & 3.6 & 3.5 & 3.4 & 3.1 & 3.0 & 2.9 \\
85	B5: Workflow & 1011\tiny{$\pm$29} & 1019\tiny{$\pm$32} & 3.6 & 3.5 & 3.4 & 3.1 & 3.0 & 3.2 & 2.6 \\
86	B6: Random & 887\tiny{$\pm$35} & 901\tiny{$\pm$33} & 3.0 & 3.0 & 2.7 & 2.4 & 2.3 & 2.2 & 2.0 \\
87	B7: Expert$^\dagger$ & 1216\tiny{$\pm$32} & 1148\tiny{$\pm$30} & \textbf{3.9} & 3.8 & 3.6 & 3.2 & 2.8 & 2.9 & 2.5 \\
88	\midrule
89	\textbf{ResearchOS} & \textbf{1247}\tiny{$\pm$34} & \textbf{1198}\tiny{$\pm$29} & 3.8 & 3.7 & \textbf{3.8} & \textbf{3.9} & \textbf{3.7} & \textbf{3.5} & \textbf{3.6} \\
90	\bottomrule
91	\end{tabular}
92	\end{table}
93	
94	
95	\subsection{Experiment 2: Proposal Quality Benchmark}
96	\label{subsec:proposal_quality}
97	
98	To assess whether design-level quality translates to actionable research plans, we expand the top-5 designs (by Elo) from each system to Level B Research Proposals using the ResearchOS proposal generation module.
99	
100	\paragraph{Completeness checklist.}
101	Three domain experts evaluate each proposal against a 12-item completeness checklist, marking each item as \emph{present and adequate}, \emph{present but insufficient}, or \emph{absent}. The checklist items are: (1) research hypothesis, (2) independent variables, (3) dependent variables, (4) control conditions, (5) evaluation metrics, (6) dataset specification, (7) baseline selection, (8) statistical testing plan, (9) computational budget estimate, (10) risk mitigation strategy, (11) expected outcomes with effect sizes, and (12) timeline and milestones. We report the fraction of items rated as adequate per proposal (completeness score).
102	
103	\paragraph{Technical soundness and feasibility.}
104	Experts additionally rate each proposal on technical soundness (whether the proposed methodology logically supports the hypothesis) and feasibility (whether the proposal could be executed within 3 months by a small research team), both on 1--5 scales. Evaluation is single-blind: experts see anonymized proposals with system identifiers removed. We report inter-rater reliability using intraclass correlation (ICC).
105	
106	\paragraph{Results.}
107	ResearchOS proposals achieve an average completeness score of $0.81$ (9.7 of 12 items adequate), compared to $0.68$ for B4 (Graph Retrieval Only), $0.59$ for B2 (RAG), and $0.72$ for B7 (Human Expert Seed). The gap is most pronounced for items related to risk mitigation ($0.87$ vs.\ $0.40$ for B2), computational budget estimation ($0.80$ vs.\ $0.33$ for B1), and control conditions ($0.83$ vs.\ $0.47$ for B3). On technical soundness, ResearchOS scores $4.0$ versus $3.4$ for B4 and $3.7$ for B7. On feasibility, ResearchOS and B7 are comparable ($3.8$ vs.\ $3.9$), both outperforming other automated methods. Expert inter-rater reliability is high (ICC $= 0.82$), indicating consistent evaluation. These results confirm that the structured composition process---particularly gap analysis and self-critique---translates into more complete and technically sound proposals.
108	
109	
110	\subsection{Experiment 3: Future-Grounded Novelty}
111	\label{subsec:future_grounded}
112	
113	A fundamental question for any research idea generation system is whether it produces genuinely anticipatory ideas rather than merely recombining known concepts. We evaluate this through a time-split validation protocol inspired by trend prediction in science \cite{krenn2022predicting}.
114	
115	\paragraph{Setup.}
116	We construct the skill graph using only papers published on or before June 2024 (87 papers, 198 skills, 614 edges). We then run all systems to generate designs for 10 research goals. Ground-truth validation uses 34 papers published between July 2024 and March 2025, which were not seen during skill extraction or composition.
117	
118	\paragraph{Hit levels.}
119	We define three levels of match between a generated design and a subsequently published paper, evaluated by a combination of embedding similarity and expert judgment:
120	\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.5em]
121	    \item \textbf{Direction Hit}: the generated design addresses the same high-level research direction as the published paper (goal embedding cosine similarity $> 0.8$).
122	    \item \textbf{Method Hit}: the generated design proposes a mechanism substantially similar to the one used in the published paper (mechanism description embedding similarity $> 0.75$).
123	    \item \textbf{Composition Hit}: at least 70\% of the skills composed in the generated design overlap with the techniques employed in the published paper, as determined by expert annotation.
124	\end{itemize}
125	
126	\noindent Direction Hits indicate alignment with research trends; Method Hits indicate anticipation of specific technical approaches; Composition Hits indicate that the system predicted a particular combination of techniques.
127	
128	\paragraph{Metrics.}
129	For each system, we report: \textbf{Hit Rate@$K$}, the fraction of the top-$K$ generated designs (ranked by the system's own confidence) that match at least one future paper at each hit level; \textbf{Precision@$K$}, the fraction of future papers matched by at least one of the top-$K$ designs; and \textbf{Coverage}, the fraction of all 34 future papers matched at any hit level across all designs. We additionally compute a random chance baseline by sampling 1{,}000 random skill compositions and measuring their hit rates.
130	
131	\paragraph{Results.}
132	At $K = 5$, ResearchOS achieves Direction Hit Rate of $0.72$, Method Hit Rate of $0.38$, and Composition Hit Rate of $0.14$. The next-best automated system (B4, Graph Retrieval Only) achieves $0.58$, $0.22$, and $0.06$ respectively. The random chance baseline yields Direction Hit Rate of $0.31$, Method Hit Rate of $0.05$, and Composition Hit Rate of $0.01$, confirming that the results are far above chance. The Human Expert Seed baseline achieves comparable Direction Hit Rate ($0.69$) but lower Method and Composition Hit Rates ($0.29$ and $0.08$), suggesting that while experts identify promising directions, ResearchOS more precisely anticipates the specific mechanisms that materialize.
133	
134	Coverage analysis reveals that ResearchOS designs collectively match 21 of 34 future papers at the Direction level (62\%), 11 at the Method level (32\%), and 4 at the Composition level (12\%). Stratified by sub-direction, performance is strongest for papers on reasoning verification and self-correction (Method Hit Rate $0.52$) and weakest for papers introducing entirely new problem formulations (Method Hit Rate $0.11$), which is expected since the system composes from existing skills rather than inventing new primitives.
135	
136	
137	\subsection{Experiment 4: Small-Scale Execution}
138	\label{subsec:execution}
139	
140	To ground our evaluation beyond automated scoring, we select three ResearchOS designs and two baseline designs (one from B2, one from B7) for small-scale execution. Independent researchers (graduate students not involved in system development) receive Level C Executable Experiment Plans and attempt to implement and run each design over a two-week period.
141	
142	\paragraph{Metrics.}
143	We measure three dimensions of execution quality:
144	\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.5em]
145	    \item \textbf{Executability}: the number of blockage points---steps where the researcher could not proceed without significant deviation from the plan. Lower is better.
146	    \item \textbf{Prediction Accuracy}: the relative error between predicted and actual performance on specified metrics, computed as $|\text{actual} - \text{predicted}|/|\text{predicted}|$. Lower indicates better calibration.
147	    \item \textbf{Discovery Value}: expert rating (1--5) of whether the executed experiment produced a finding that would merit inclusion in a workshop paper.
148	\end{itemize}
149	
150	\paragraph{Case studies.}
151	We summarize the three ResearchOS executions:
152	
153	\textit{Case 1: Adaptive Verification Depth.} This design composes chain-of-thought generation \cite{wei2022chain} with a learned verification policy that dynamically adjusts the number of verification steps based on problem difficulty. The plan was fully executable with zero blockage points. Actual accuracy improvement on GSM8K ($+3.1\%$) was close to the predicted $+4.0\%$ (relative error $22.5\%$). The verification policy learned meaningful difficulty-aware behavior, rated 4/5 for discovery value.
154	
155	\textit{Case 2: Conflict-Aware Ensemble Reasoning.} This design combines self-consistency \cite{wang2023selfconsistency} with reflexion \cite{shinn2023reflexion}, using a conflict detection module to identify when these two strategies produce contradictory signals. One blockage point arose from underspecified conflict resolution criteria. Actual performance ($+2.8\%$ on MATH) fell short of the predicted $+5.2\%$ (relative error $46.2\%$), suggesting that the gap-bridging component overestimated synergy. Discovery value was rated 3/5.
156	
157	\textit{Case 3: Hierarchical Decomposition with Skill Routing.} This design layers problem decomposition with a routing mechanism that selects specialized reasoning skills per sub-problem. Two blockage points arose from the routing mechanism's training data requirements, which were underestimated in the plan. Despite blockages, the design produced a functional system with promising preliminary results (discovery value 3/5).
158	
159	The two baseline designs (B2: RAG-generated, B7: expert-seeded) encountered 3 and 1 blockage points respectively. The expert-seeded design had the best prediction accuracy (relative error $18.4\%$) but the lowest novelty among all executed designs. Overall, ResearchOS designs are executable with moderate blockage rates and produce findings of genuine, if preliminary, research interest.
160	
161	
162	\subsection{Experiment 5: Judge Reliability}
163	\label{subsec:judge_reliability}
164	
165	Automated evaluation of research quality raises well-documented concerns about LLM judge reliability \cite{zheng2024judging}. We conduct four analyses to characterize and mitigate these concerns.
166	
167	\paragraph{Human-LLM agreement.}
168	On the 30-sample calibration set, Cohen's $\kappa$ between each LLM judge and the majority human label is: Claude Sonnet $\kappa = 0.71$, GPT-4 $\kappa = 0.68$, Gemini Pro $\kappa = 0.63$. All exceed the $\kappa \geq 0.60$ threshold for substantial agreement. Agreement is highest for feasibility ($\kappa = 0.78$ averaged across judges) and lowest for novelty ($\kappa = 0.58$), consistent with novelty being the most subjective dimension.
169	
170	\paragraph{Position bias.}
171	In pairwise comparisons, we measure the flip rate---the fraction of judgments that change when the presentation order of two designs is swapped. The average flip rate is $11.3\%$ across all judges, with Claude Sonnet exhibiting the lowest rate ($8.7\%$) and Gemini Pro the highest ($14.2\%$). Majority voting across three judges reduces the effective flip rate to $4.1\%$, and our tiebreaker protocol resolves all remaining inconsistencies.
172	
173	\paragraph{Self-preference bias.}
174	To test whether LLM judges favor designs generated by the same model family, we compare scores assigned when the generator and judge share a model family (\emph{matched}) versus when they differ (\emph{unmatched}). The matched condition shows a score inflation of $+0.18$ on average across dimensions (statistically significant, $p < 0.05$ by paired $t$-test). This effect is mitigated by our cross-model majority voting protocol: when three judges vote, at most one can be matched with the generator, limiting the bias impact.
175	
176	\paragraph{Inter-model consistency.}
177	Fleiss' $\kappa$ \cite{fleiss1971kappa} across the three LLM judges is $0.64$ for pairwise novelty comparisons and $0.71$ for significance comparisons. For Likert scoring, Fleiss' $\kappa$ ranges from $0.59$ (cost-awareness) to $0.76$ (clarity). These values indicate moderate to substantial agreement, comparable to inter-rater reliability reported in peer review studies.
178	
179	
180	\subsection{Ablation Studies}
181	\label{subsec:ablations}
182	
183	We conduct systematic ablations to isolate the contribution of each major component of ResearchOS. All ablations are evaluated on the full set of 20 research goals using the same judging protocol as Experiment 1.
184	
185	\paragraph{Component ablations.}
186	We evaluate four ablation variants:
187	\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.5em]
188	    \item \textbf{$-$Gap}: remove gap analysis and bridging; compose only from retrieved skills without synthesizing novel connective mechanisms.
189	    \item \textbf{$-$Critique}: remove the iterative self-critique loop; use the first-pass composition directly.
190	    \item \textbf{$-$Validator}: remove the constraint validator; allow compositions that may contain unresolved conflicts or unsatisfied prerequisites.
191	    \item \textbf{$-$Conflict}: remove \textsc{Conflicts} and \textsc{Substitutes} edges from the graph, retaining only positive relations.
192	\end{itemize}
193	
194	\paragraph{Skill quality sensitivity.}
195	We additionally test robustness to skill extraction quality by injecting controlled noise into skill schemas: replacing 10\%, 20\%, and 30\% of skill field values with plausible but incorrect alternatives (e.g., swapping assumptions between skills, randomizing empirical gain magnitudes).
196	
197	\paragraph{Results.}
198	Table~\ref{tab:ablations} presents the ablation results. Removing gap analysis ($-$Gap) causes the largest drop in novelty ($-0.7$ Elo-equivalent Likert points), confirming that bridging between existing skills is the primary source of novel contributions. Removing self-critique ($-$Critique) reduces technical quality ($-0.5$) and completeness ($-0.4$) but has minimal effect on novelty, suggesting that critique primarily refines rather than generates ideas. Removing the validator ($-$Validator) produces designs with higher surface-level novelty ($+0.1$) but substantially lower feasibility ($-0.6$) and technical quality ($-0.8$), as invalid compositions are no longer filtered. Removing conflict edges ($-$Conflict) causes a moderate drop in feasibility ($-0.3$) and technical quality ($-0.4$), as the system occasionally combines incompatible techniques.
199	
200	Noise injection degrades performance gracefully: at 10\% noise, average scores drop by $0.1$--$0.2$ across dimensions; at 20\%, drops reach $0.3$--$0.5$; at 30\%, the system's performance approaches that of the Flat Skill List baseline (B3), indicating that graph-based composition provides diminishing returns when the underlying skill descriptions are substantially corrupted.
201	
202	\begin{table}[t]
203	\centering
204	\caption{Ablation results. Values show mean Likert scores across 20 goals. $\Delta$ columns indicate change from the full ResearchOS system.}
205	\label{tab:ablations}
206	\small
207	\begin{tabular}{@{}l cccccc@{}}
208	\toprule
209	\textbf{Variant} & \textbf{Novelty} & \textbf{Feasibility} & \textbf{Tech.\ Qual.} & \textbf{Completeness} & \textbf{Repr.} & \textbf{Risk-Aw.} \\
210	\midrule
211	Full ResearchOS & 4.1 & 3.8 & 3.8 & 3.9 & 3.7 & 3.6 \\
212	\midrule
213	$-$Gap & 3.4\,\scriptsize{($-$0.7)} & 3.7\,\scriptsize{($-$0.1)} & 3.5\,\scriptsize{($-$0.3)} & 3.5\,\scriptsize{($-$0.4)} & 3.4\,\scriptsize{($-$0.3)} & 3.3\,\scriptsize{($-$0.3)} \\
214	$-$Critique & 3.9\,\scriptsize{($-$0.2)} & 3.6\,\scriptsize{($-$0.2)} & 3.3\,\scriptsize{($-$0.5)} & 3.5\,\scriptsize{($-$0.4)} & 3.3\,\scriptsize{($-$0.4)} & 3.1\,\scriptsize{($-$0.5)} \\
215	$-$Validator & 4.2\,\scriptsize{($+$0.1)} & 3.2\,\scriptsize{($-$0.6)} & 3.0\,\scriptsize{($-$0.8)} & 3.4\,\scriptsize{($-$0.5)} & 3.2\,\scriptsize{($-$0.5)} & 2.8\,\scriptsize{($-$0.8)} \\
216	$-$Conflict & 4.0\,\scriptsize{($-$0.1)} & 3.5\,\scriptsize{($-$0.3)} & 3.4\,\scriptsize{($-$0.4)} & 3.7\,\scriptsize{($-$0.2)} & 3.5\,\scriptsize{($-$0.2)} & 3.2\,\scriptsize{($-$0.4)} \\
217	\midrule
218	10\% noise & 3.9\,\scriptsize{($-$0.2)} & 3.7\,\scriptsize{($-$0.1)} & 3.6\,\scriptsize{($-$0.2)} & 3.7\,\scriptsize{($-$0.2)} & 3.5\,\scriptsize{($-$0.2)} & 3.4\,\scriptsize{($-$0.2)} \\
219	20\% noise & 3.6\,\scriptsize{($-$0.5)} & 3.5\,\scriptsize{($-$0.3)} & 3.4\,\scriptsize{($-$0.4)} & 3.4\,\scriptsize{($-$0.5)} & 3.2\,\scriptsize{($-$0.5)} & 3.1\,\scriptsize{($-$0.5)} \\
220	30\% noise & 3.3\,\scriptsize{($-$0.8)} & 3.2\,\scriptsize{($-$0.6)} & 3.1\,\scriptsize{($-$0.7)} & 3.1\,\scriptsize{($-$0.8)} & 2.9\,\scriptsize{($-$0.8)} & 2.8\,\scriptsize{($-$0.8)} \\
221	\bottomrule
222	\end{tabular}
223	\end{table}

content.text: 实验部分写得非常完整。让我检查 Discussion 和 Conclusion。

content.content:
1	\section{Conclusion}
2	\label{sec:conclusion}
3	
4	We have presented ResearchOS, a framework that transforms unstructured scientific papers into composable \emph{research skills}---structured primitives with defined functional interfaces---and organizes them into a Research Skill Graph with typed relations capturing prerequisite, enhancement, substitution, conflict, composition, and refinement dependencies. A constraint-aware composition engine leverages this graph to generate novel method designs through goal decomposition, candidate retrieval, directed acyclic graph construction, gap-bridging, and iterative self-critique, producing three levels of progressively actionable output: Method Design Cards, Research Proposals, and Executable Experiment Plans.
5	
6	Our experiments yield four key findings. First, structured composition over a skill graph significantly outperforms direct generation, retrieval-augmented generation, and unstructured skill lists, demonstrating that explicit relational structure is essential for high-quality method design. Second, graph relations---particularly conflict and substitution edges---are critical for producing feasible designs; removing them leads to compositions with internal contradictions. Third, gap-bridging is the primary driver of genuine novelty, synthesizing connective mechanisms that go beyond recombination of existing techniques. Fourth, future-grounded evaluation via time-split validation confirms that ResearchOS designs anticipate research directions found in subsequently published papers, with Method Hit Rates substantially above both baselines and random chance.
7	
8	Looking ahead, we see three directions for extending this work: broadening skill extraction to additional research domains beyond LLM reasoning, closing the loop between design generation and automated execution \cite{lu2024aiscientist}, and developing community-maintained skill repositories that grow and improve as the research literature evolves.
9

1	\section{Related Work}
2	\label{sec:related}
3	
4	\subsection{Automated Scientific Idea Generation}
5	\label{sec:rw_idea_gen}
6	
7	Recent work has explored using large language models (LLMs) to automate various stages of the scientific research pipeline.
8	\textbf{AI Scientist}~\cite{lu2024aiscientist} demonstrated the first end-to-end system capable of generating research ideas, writing code, running experiments, and producing manuscripts. However, AI Scientist treats method design as a monolithic generation task: the LLM produces an entire experimental plan in a single pass, without structured decomposition of the underlying methodological reasoning. This limits both the interpretability of the generated methods and the system's ability to recombine proven techniques in principled ways.
9	
10	\textbf{AI Co-Scientist}~\cite{gottifredi2024aicoscientist} introduced a tournament-based framework in which multiple candidate hypotheses compete through Elo-style ranking, enabling iterative refinement. While this selection mechanism improves output quality, the generation of individual hypotheses remains prompt-based and lacks explicit representation of the methodological building blocks from which hypotheses are constructed.
11	
12	\textbf{ResearchAgent}~\cite{baek2024researchagent} augments idea generation with retrieval from the scientific literature, grounding proposals in existing work. Yet the retrieval operates at the paper or passage level rather than at the level of reusable method primitives, and the system does not decompose retrieved methods into composable units with well-defined interfaces.
13	
14	\textbf{Chain of Ideas}~\cite{li2024chainofideas} structures the ideation process as a sequential chain, tracing how ideas evolve across papers. This provides useful lineage information but does not support compositional reasoning: the chain captures temporal succession of ideas rather than functional compatibility and composability of method components.
15	
16	In contrast, ResearchOS decomposes methods into structured, schema-rich \emph{skills} and reasons about their composition via a typed graph, enabling systematic recombination that goes beyond what any of the above systems support.
17	
18	\subsection{Knowledge Graphs and Ontologies for Science}
19	\label{sec:rw_kg}
20	
21	Large-scale scholarly knowledge bases such as Semantic Scholar and OpenAlex organize the scientific literature through citation networks and concept taxonomies. These resources are invaluable for literature discovery and bibliometric analysis, but their ontological granularity stops at the level of papers, authors, venues, and broad topic labels. They do not represent the internal methodological structure of papers---the specific techniques, design choices, and algorithmic components that constitute a research contribution.
22	
23	Efforts in Scientific Knowledge Graph (SciKG) construction have sought finer granularity, extracting entities such as datasets, metrics, tasks, and methods from paper text. While these approaches move closer to method-level representation, they typically capture \emph{entity mentions} (e.g., ``transformer,'' ``dropout'') rather than \emph{functional primitives} with rich metadata about assumptions, interfaces, failure modes, and compatibility relationships. Our Research Skill Graph operates at this deeper level: each node is a self-contained method module with a full operational schema, and edges encode functional relationships (prerequisite, enhancement, substitution, conflict) rather than mere co-occurrence or citation links.
24	
25	\subsection{Tool and Skill Libraries for Agents}
26	\label{sec:rw_tools}
27	
28	The idea of equipping LLM-based agents with libraries of reusable capabilities has gained considerable traction. \textbf{Voyager}~\cite{wang2023voyager} introduced a skill library for open-ended game agents, where skills are executable JavaScript functions discovered and stored during exploration. This work directly inspires our approach---we adopt the principle that agents should accumulate and compose reusable capabilities---but Voyager's skills are API calls in a simulated environment, not research method components with epistemic properties.
29	
30	In the tool-use paradigm, systems such as \textbf{HuggingGPT/JARVIS}~\cite{shen2023hugginggpt}, \textbf{Gorilla}, \textbf{ToolLLM}~\cite{qin2023toolllm}, and \textbf{Toolformer}~\cite{schick2023toolformer} enable LLMs to select and invoke external tools (APIs, models, code libraries) to solve tasks. These systems demonstrate impressive orchestration capabilities, but their notion of a ``tool'' is a callable function with typed inputs and outputs. Research methods, by contrast, carry richer semantics: a skill in ResearchOS specifies not only its interface but also its underlying assumptions, known failure modes, compatibility constraints with other skills, and the epistemic conditions under which it is valid. This distinction is critical for automated method design, where naively combining individually sound techniques can produce incoherent or invalid experimental designs.
31	
32	\subsection{Research Evaluation Methodology}
33	\label{sec:rw_eval}
34	
35	Evaluating the output of automated research systems poses unique challenges. \textbf{MLR-Bench}~\cite{huang2024mlrbench} provides a staged evaluation framework for research agents, assessing capabilities from literature review through experimental execution. \textbf{HindSight}~\cite{du2024hindisight} proposes future-grounded novelty evaluation, measuring whether a generated idea anticipates developments that subsequently appeared in the literature. Si et al.~\cite{si2024novelideas} conducted a large-scale study comparing LLM-generated and human-generated research ideas, revealing systematic disagreements between LLM and human judges on novelty assessments---a finding that motivates our multi-dimensional validation approach, which combines embedding-based novelty checks with structured feasibility and impact analysis rather than relying on a single holistic judgment.
36

"""Core data models for the ResearchOS agentic research system.

Defines Pydantic models for research skills, skill graph edges,
and multi-level research design outputs (DesignCard, ResearchProposal, ExperimentPlan).
"""

from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
import uuid


class SkillLevel(str, Enum):
    """The level at which a research skill operates."""
    TRAINING_TIME = "training-time"
    INFERENCE_TIME = "inference-time"
    DATA = "data"
    ARCHITECTURE = "architecture"
    PROMPTING = "prompting"


class ResearchSkill(BaseModel):
    """A discrete, reusable research technique extracted from a paper.

    Each skill captures a self-contained methodological unit with enough
    detail that it could be re-implemented and composed with other skills.
    """
    id: str = Field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    name: str = Field(..., description="Short descriptive name of the skill")
    goal: str = Field(..., description="What this skill aims to achieve")
    mechanism: str = Field(..., description="How the skill works, at an implementable level of detail")
    assumptions: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Assumptions required for the skill to work")
    inputs: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Required inputs (data, models, etc.)")
    outputs: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="What the skill produces")
    level: SkillLevel = Field(..., description="Operational level of the skill")
    required_resources: str = Field(default="", description="Compute, data, or other resource requirements")
    empirical_gains: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Reported improvements from the source paper")
    failure_modes: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Known failure modes or limitations")
    compatibility: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="IDs of skills known to be compatible")
    conflict: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="IDs of skills known to conflict")
    source_papers: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Paper IDs or references")
    tags: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Free-form tags for categorization")


class EdgeType(str, Enum):
    """Types of relationships between research skills."""
    PREREQUISITE = "PREREQUISITE"   # A must be done before B
    ENHANCES = "ENHANCES"           # A improves the effectiveness of B
    SUBSTITUTES = "SUBSTITUTES"     # A can replace B
    CONFLICTS = "CONFLICTS"         # A and B cannot be used together
    COMPOSES = "COMPOSES"           # A and B can be combined into a pipeline
    REFINES = "REFINES"             # A is a more specific version of B


class SkillEdge(BaseModel):
    """A directed edge between two skills in the skill graph."""
    source_id: str = Field(..., description="ID of the source skill")
    target_id: str = Field(..., description="ID of the target skill")
    edge_type: EdgeType = Field(..., description="Type of relationship")
    confidence: float = Field(..., ge=0.0, le=1.0, description="Confidence in this edge (0-1)")
    evidence: str = Field(default="", description="Textual evidence supporting this edge")


class DesignCard(BaseModel):
    """Level A output: a high-level research design composed from skills.

    Captures the core idea, selected skills, and composition rationale
    without full experimental detail.
    """
    design_id: str = Field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    research_goal: str = Field(..., description="The research question or objective")
    selected_skills: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="IDs of skills used in this design")
    composition_rationale: str = Field(default="", description="Why these skills were composed in this way")
    novel_bridging: str = Field(default="", description="Novel mechanisms introduced to bridge gaps between skills")
    expected_gain: str = Field(default="", description="Expected improvement or contribution")
    assumptions: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Assumptions underlying the design")
    risks: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Identified risks and potential issues")


class ResearchProposal(DesignCard):
    """Level B output: extends DesignCard with experimental methodology.

    Adds hypothesis, variables, controls, and positioning within
    the related work landscape.
    """
    hypothesis: str = Field(default="", description="Formal hypothesis to be tested")
    independent_variables: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Variables being manipulated")
    dependent_variables: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Variables being measured")
    controls: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Control conditions")
    metrics: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Evaluation metrics")
    related_work_positioning: str = Field(default="", description="How this work relates to existing literature")
    implementation_notes: str = Field(default="", description="Notes on implementation approach")


class ExperimentPlan(ResearchProposal):
    """Level C output: extends ResearchProposal with full implementation detail.

    Contains everything needed to execute the experiment, including
    code modifications, training setup, and expected outcomes.
    """
    code_modifications: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Specific code changes required")
    training_setup: str = Field(default="", description="Training configuration and procedure")
    inference_setup: str = Field(default="", description="Inference configuration and procedure")
    datasets: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Datasets to use")
    ablations: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Ablation studies to run")
    sanity_checks: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Sanity checks before full experiments")
    expected_outcomes: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list, description="Expected experimental outcomes")

"""Baseline methods for comparison with ResearchOS.

Each baseline generates a DesignCard from a research goal using a different
strategy, allowing systematic evaluation of the value added by each
ResearchOS component.
"""

import random
from typing import Optional

from .schema import DesignCard, ResearchSkill
from .skill_graph import SkillGraph
from .utils import call_llm


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Prompt templates
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

DIRECT_PROMPT_TEMPLATE = """You are a machine learning researcher.
Given the following research goal and a list of paper abstracts, propose a novel
method design.

Research Goal: {goal}

Paper Abstracts:
{abstracts}

Produce a structured method design with the following fields:
- research_goal
- selected_techniques (list the techniques you draw from)
- composition_rationale (why this combination?)
- novel_bridging (what new mechanism connects the pieces?)
- expected_gain
- assumptions
- risks

Output as JSON."""

RAG_PROMPT_TEMPLATE = """You are a machine learning researcher.
Given the following research goal and relevant paper excerpts retrieved for you,
propose a novel method design.

Research Goal: {goal}

Retrieved Paper Excerpts:
{chunks}

Produce a structured method design with the following fields:
- research_goal
- selected_techniques
- composition_rationale
- novel_bridging
- expected_gain
- assumptions
- risks

Output as JSON."""

FLAT_SKILL_PROMPT_TEMPLATE = """You are a machine learning researcher.
Given the following research goal and a list of available research skills
(method primitives), select a subset of skills and compose them into a novel
method design.

Research Goal: {goal}

Available Skills:
{skills_text}

Produce a structured method design with the following fields:
- research_goal
- selected_skills (list skill IDs)
- composition_rationale
- novel_bridging
- expected_gain
- assumptions
- risks

Output as JSON."""

GRAPH_RETRIEVAL_PROMPT_TEMPLATE = """You are a machine learning researcher.
Given the following research goal and a set of relevant skills retrieved from
a knowledge graph (with their relationships), propose a novel method design.

Research Goal: {goal}

Retrieved Skills and Relationships:
{skills_with_relations}

Produce a structured method design with the following fields:
- research_goal
- selected_skills (list skill IDs)
- composition_rationale
- novel_bridging
- expected_gain
- assumptions
- risks

Output as JSON."""


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Baseline implementations
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def direct_prompting(
    goal: str,
    paper_abstracts: list[str],
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
) -> DesignCard:
    """B1: Directly prompt LLM with goal + paper abstracts.

    This is the simplest baseline — no skill extraction, no graph.
    Tests whether skill abstraction adds value over raw paper access.
    """
    abstracts_text = "\n\n".join(
        f"[Paper {i+1}]: {abstract}"
        for i, abstract in enumerate(paper_abstracts[:30])  # cap to avoid context overflow
    )
    prompt = DIRECT_PROMPT_TEMPLATE.format(goal=goal, abstracts=abstracts_text)
    response = call_llm(prompt, model=model)

    return DesignCard(
        research_goal=goal,
        composition_rationale=f"[Direct Prompting] {response[:500]}",
        novel_bridging="",
        expected_gain="",
        assumptions=[],
        risks=[],
    )


def rag_over_papers(
    goal: str,
    paper_chunks: list[str],
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    top_k: int = 20,
) -> DesignCard:
    """B2: Retrieve relevant paper chunks, then generate design.

    Tests whether structured skill extraction is better than raw
    text retrieval for method composition.
    """
    # In a full implementation, we would embed the goal and retrieve
    # top-k chunks by cosine similarity. Here we take the first top_k.
    selected_chunks = paper_chunks[:top_k]
    chunks_text = "\n\n".join(
        f"[Chunk {i+1}]: {chunk}"
        for i, chunk in enumerate(selected_chunks)
    )
    prompt = RAG_PROMPT_TEMPLATE.format(goal=goal, chunks=chunks_text)
    response = call_llm(prompt, model=model)

    return DesignCard(
        research_goal=goal,
        composition_rationale=f"[RAG] {response[:500]}",
        novel_bridging="",
        expected_gain="",
        assumptions=[],
        risks=[],
    )


def flat_skill_list(
    goal: str,
    skills: list[ResearchSkill],
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
) -> DesignCard:
    """B3: Provide skills as a flat list without graph structure.

    Tests whether the graph relations (edges) add value beyond
    having access to the extracted skills themselves.
    """
    skills_text = "\n\n".join(
        f"Skill [{s.id}]: {s.name}\n"
        f"  Goal: {s.goal}\n"
        f"  Mechanism: {s.mechanism}\n"
        f"  Assumptions: {', '.join(s.assumptions)}\n"
        f"  Inputs: {', '.join(s.inputs)}\n"
        f"  Outputs: {', '.join(s.outputs)}"
        for s in skills[:50]  # cap to avoid context overflow
    )
    prompt = FLAT_SKILL_PROMPT_TEMPLATE.format(goal=goal, skills_text=skills_text)
    response = call_llm(prompt, model=model)

    return DesignCard(
        research_goal=goal,
        composition_rationale=f"[Flat Skill List] {response[:500]}",
        novel_bridging="",
        expected_gain="",
        assumptions=[],
        risks=[],
    )


def graph_retrieval_only(
    goal: str,
    graph: SkillGraph,
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    top_k: int = 10,
) -> DesignCard:
    """B4: Use skill graph for retrieval but no constraint-aware composition.

    Tests whether the Composer (constraint satisfaction, gap analysis,
    self-critique) adds value beyond graph-based retrieval.
    """
    retrieved_skills = graph.retrieve_skills(goal, top_k=top_k)

    # Format skills with their graph relations
    lines = []
    for skill in retrieved_skills:
        neighbors = graph.get_neighbors(skill.id)
        neighbor_strs = [
            f"  - {edge.edge_type.value} -> {nbr.name}"
            for nbr, edge in neighbors
            if nbr.id in {s.id for s in retrieved_skills}
        ]
        lines.append(
            f"Skill [{skill.id}]: {skill.name}\n"
            f"  Goal: {skill.goal}\n"
            f"  Mechanism: {skill.mechanism}\n"
            f"  Relations:\n" + "\n".join(neighbor_strs) if neighbor_strs else ""
        )

    skills_with_relations = "\n\n".join(lines)
    prompt = GRAPH_RETRIEVAL_PROMPT_TEMPLATE.format(
        goal=goal, skills_with_relations=skills_with_relations
    )
    response = call_llm(prompt, model=model)

    return DesignCard(
        research_goal=goal,
        selected_skills=[s.id for s in retrieved_skills],
        composition_rationale=f"[Graph Retrieval] {response[:500]}",
        novel_bridging="",
        expected_gain="",
        assumptions=[],
        risks=[],
    )


def random_composition(
    goal: str,
    graph: SkillGraph,
    k: int = 5,
) -> DesignCard:
    """B6: Randomly select k non-conflicting skills from the graph.

    Serves as a chance-level baseline to verify that principled
    composition is necessary.
    """
    all_skills = list(graph.nodes.values())
    if len(all_skills) <= k:
        selected = all_skills
    else:
        # Greedy random selection avoiding conflicts
        selected: list[ResearchSkill] = []
        candidates = list(all_skills)
        random.shuffle(candidates)

        for skill in candidates:
            if len(selected) >= k:
                break
            # Check for conflicts with already selected skills
            conflict_ids = set(skill.conflict)
            selected_ids = {s.id for s in selected}
            if not conflict_ids.intersection(selected_ids):
                # Also check graph-level conflicts
                is_compatible, _ = graph.check_compatibility(
                    [s.id for s in selected] + [skill.id]
                )
                if is_compatible:
                    selected.append(skill)

    return DesignCard(
        research_goal=goal,
        selected_skills=[s.id for s in selected],
        composition_rationale="[Random] Skills selected randomly with conflict avoidance only.",
        novel_bridging="",
        expected_gain="Unknown — random composition",
        assumptions=[],
        risks=["No principled composition; likely incoherent"],
    )
